Global Data Centres and Electricity Consumption

Data centres for artificial intelligence and other forms of computing account for a small share of worldwide electricity consumption, according to estimates prepared by the International Energy Agency (IEA) and other researchers.

Consumption is growing very rapidly but from a low base and the extra demand will be dwarfed by increases from industrial users, electric vehicles, domestic appliances and airconditioning over the next decade.

From a global perspective, increased electricity demand from data centres is likely to have only a moderate impact on prices, service and reliability for other users.

But data centres are strongly clustered in a small number of countries, and within them a small number of regions, a pattern that is expected to persist.

In these regions, data centres account for a high and growing share of consumption, having a much greater impact on other users.

Data centres and artificial intelligence are expected to pose a relatively modest challenge for the global energy system in the future.

But managing their local impacts on availability, cost and reliability for other users is already proving a significant challenge ...

RE: Hormuz offset flows

An error I still see people making months into tracking Hormuz oil flow is thinking you can add the entire ~7.5 MMbpd of combined Saudi Red Sea + UAE Fujairah flow as an offset to get back to that ~20 MMbpd pre-war Hormuz flow level.

But those offset routes didn't start at zero and were shipping ~3.5 MMbpd of oil prewar.

You get to add ~4 MMbpd to Hormuz flow from offsets, not 7+ MMbpd.

EIA weekly petroleum status: US crude inventories fell by 1.7 million barrels, while a further 3 million barrels were released from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR), taking the cumulative post-war drawdown to almost 100 million barrels. Gasoline stocks declined by 1.5 million barrels, with Gulf Coast inventories falling to their lowest level since 2017, highlighting continued regional tightness during the peak summer driving season. Distillate inventories, however, rose by 4.6 million barrels, providing some welcome relief to the segment of the refined products market that has experienced the greatest supply stress in recent months. 
Elsewhere, inventories at the Cushing delivery hub climbed back above 20 million barrels. US crude exports increased to 3.7 million bpd but remained below the one-year average of 4.2 million bpd and well below the record 6.4 million bpd reached in May. 
Domestic crude production was unchanged, while four-week average implied demand for both gasoline and distillates eased, broadly in line with normal seasonal trends.

Morgan Stanley: All iPhone 18 series may increase in price by US$200, Apple's profits are expected to be boosted Morgan Stanley said in its latest research report that the tight supply of global memory chips has pushed up component costs, but Apple, with its strong pricing power, is expected to protect profit margins through product price increases and further increase revenue and earnings per share.

The bank maintained its "overweight" rating and target price of $360 for Apple. Morgan Stanley predicts that Apple’s increase in selling prices for each product line may drive third-quarter earnings per share 2% to 4% higher than current expectations, and drive full-year earnings per share in fiscal 2027 by about 1%.

Although Apple has not yet announced the pricing of the iPhone 18 series, the bank believes that the possibility of a unified price increase of US$200 for all models is rising. The research report pointed out that demand for Apple products is relatively insensitive to price changes.

The historical price elasticity of iPhone is only 0.2 to 0.5, which means that if the selling price increases by 10%, shipments may only decrease by 2% to 5%; the price elasticity of Mac and iPad is about 0.8 to 1.0.

After Apple raised the prices of MacBook and iPad in June this year, the delivery cycle of related products did not change significantly, indicating that the price increase has not significantly curbed demand. Cost pressure mainly comes from the memory shortage caused by the AI boom.

Morgan Stanley predicts that the average cost of DRAM purchased by Apple will increase by approximately 370% from fiscal 2025 to fiscal 2027, and the cost of NAND will increase by approximately 280% during the same period. If memory prices continue to remain high, Apple may raise prices again in fiscal year 2028.

The bank believes that Apple's brand loyalty, supply chain bargaining power, and supply pressure faced by competitors make it easier to pass costs on to consumers.

In addition to price increases, the new generation of Siri with intelligent capabilities and the foldable screen iPhone are also expected to promote the long-term AI replacement cycle and support iPhone demand until 2028. Nokia wrote in a press release that the technology is based on Nokia's AI-native anyRAN software and NVIDIA's Aerial AI-RAN platform, which can achieve a leap in network performance and economic benefits. HSBC: AI’s employment impact has been exaggerated, and “job exposure” does not mean “job disappearance” HSBC’s latest research report pointed out that there is a systematic bias in the current discussion about the impact of artificial intelligence on the job market.

Many studies overestimate the immediate impact of AI on employment and the risks of white-collar jobs, while underestimating the role of AI in assisting employees and improving productivity.

According to the research report, a large number of AI employment studies follow the calculation methods of a few papers, split the occupation into a number of tasks or abilities, and then measure the degree of job exposure based on the proportion of tasks that can be affected by AI.

This method ignores difficult-to-quantify work content such as interpersonal communication, responsibility assumption, and coordination and judgment, so it is especially easy to exaggerate the possibility of white-collar jobs being replaced. More importantly, “exposure to AI” generally does not mean being replaced by AI.

Some studies define AI as being able to shorten task completion time by at least 50%, which is defined as the highest exposure level; other indicators only judge whether AI can be used for a certain task, and do not distinguish whether AI is a replacement for labor or an auxiliary tool.

This difference is often ignored when relevant data are relayed by the media. HSBC believes that the mission itself is not fixed. The popularization of the Internet has eliminated old tasks such as sending letters, but created new tasks such as email.

Similarly, while AI reduces data entry work, it may increase responsibilities such as result verification, model supervision, and customer communication. Industries with high AI usage, such as technology, law, and finance, are more likely to experience task reconfiguration rather than the overall disappearance of jobs.

Public concerns have increased significantly. More than 50% of Americans are worried that they or a family member will lose their job due to AI, and more than 60% of British workers are worried about the economic impact of AI layoffs.

However, HSBC believes that the actual scale of unemployment may be significantly lower than the level suggested by the relevant headlines.

Some positions and entry-level positions may still be under pressure, but the more important impact of AI on the labor market will be to enhance the capabilities of workers rather than to replace white-collar employees on a large scale.

Goldman Sachs: European natural gas market is tighter than crude oil, TTF may need to rise to 65 euros to curb Asian demand Goldman Sachs pointed out in its latest research report that the escalation of conflicts in the Middle East has further exposed the supply vulnerability of European natural gas and the global liquefied natural gas (LNG) market.

In the past week, the European benchmark natural gas futures TTF rose to 52 euros/MWh, a cumulative increase of 78% during the year, significantly exceeding the 36% increase of Brent crude oil during the same period.

Natural gas prices performed stronger, mainly because Persian Gulf crude oil exports recovered faster than LNG, while LNG demand in Asia continued to rebound. China's LNG imports are back above last year's levels, while crude oil imports remain weak.

Data show that as of July 12, Persian Gulf crude oil exports have returned to 58% of normal levels, and LNG exports have only recovered to 19%; China's LNG imports increased by about 3% year-on-year, while crude oil imports fell by 52% year-on-year.

As the number of LNG ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz declines again, Qatar's LNG shipments, which have recently recovered slightly slower than expected, may further fall, increasing the risk of insufficient LNG imports in Europe.

At the same time, the Asian spot LNG price JKM is still attractive enough relative to TTF, which may prompt flexible allocation of US LNG cargoes to Asia instead of Europe.

Goldman Sachs said that natural gas inventories in northwest Europe are still at low levels, and the resumption of LNG exports from the Middle East has been delayed, forcing the market to find solutions for winter replenishment in advance.

Referring to this round of shocks and the European energy crisis in 2022, TTF may need to rise to about 65 euros/MWh, or 22 US dollars/million British thermal units, to push JKM to the mid-20s, significantly suppressing Asian demand.

The current TTF price of 52 euros is higher than Goldman Sachs' baseline forecast of 42 euros in the third quarter, which means that the market believes that the probability that Europe may need to rely on price to suppress LNG demand in Asia is about 45%; if it needs to destroy Asian demand more broadly, the implied probability of TTF rising to 80 euros is about 28%.

Goldman Sachs recommends that natural gas users hedge risks in the winter of 2026-2027 to prevent sharp rises in TTF and JKM.

Bank of America: Intelligent AI increases the importance of CPUs, and the demand for servers and consumer electronics accelerates differentiation BofA Securities pointed out in its latest research report that the semiconductor market is experiencing sharp differentiation: in 2026, shipments of personal computers and smartphones are both expected to fall by

more than 10% to more than 15% year-on-year, but demand for AI server CPUs remains strong, with industry shipments expected to grow by more than 30% and average selling prices rising by more than 20%.

Bank of America believes that as the popularity of intelligent AI accelerates, the bottleneck of the AI system is gradually shifting from pure GPU computing power to CPU.

Tool invocation, code execution, task orchestration, KV cache management and other links are highly dependent on the CPU, and their performance directly affects GPU idle time, response delay and overall machine throughput.

High-frequency CPUs are more suitable for low-latency tasks, while high-core CPUs are conducive to concurrent processing and rack-level throughput. Both types of architectures will benefit in the future.

Bank of America predicts that the server CPU market size will increase to approximately US$170 billion in 2030, an increase of nearly four times from US$35.2 billion in 2025, with a compound growth rate of approximately 37%; AI computing nodes and intelligent independent nodes each contribute approximately US$70 billion, and AI CPUs will account for more than 80%.

By then, the share of Arm architecture in terms of value is expected to rise to 50%, AMD will be about 26%, and Intel will drop to 24%.

In terms of individual stocks, Bank of America expects AMD's second-quarter results and third-quarter guidance to exceed expectations, benefiting from the increase in EPYC server CPU share, strong cloud demand, and the start of shipments of the MI455X "Helios" rack, and raised the target price from US$550 to US$620.

Although Intel is facing a decline in PC sales, it is expected to be offset by price increases of servers and PC processors, AI demand and improvement in product profit margins.

Arm is still hampered by the weakness of the smartphone market in the short term, and its main server CPU opportunities may be concentrated in the second half of 2026 to 2027; its potential demand for AGI CPUs in fiscal year 2027 to 2028 exceeds US$2 billion, but the existing supply is only about US$1 billion.

Although Qualcomm has raised its non-mobile phone revenue target for fiscal year 2029 to US$40 billion, including US$15 billion from data center business, it is still affected by rising storage prices, weak demand for mobile phones in China, and substitution of Apple's self-developed baseband in the short term.

Bank of America believes that server CPUs are becoming a new beneficiary link for AI infrastructure, while demand for traditional consumer electronics chips still lacks obvious recovery momentum. Morgan Stanley: U.S.

data centers encounter local resistance and project cost and schedule pressures rise Morgan Stanley’s latest research report points out that as the construction of artificial intelligence infrastructure accelerates, opposition to data centers in local communities in the United States is heating up, and electricity prices, environment and quality of life issues have become new bottlenecks for project implementation.

Third-party data shows that approximately US$156 billion in data center projects in the United States will be canceled or postponed in 2025, and approximately US$130 billion in projects will be affected in the first quarter of 2026. Actual AI capital expenditures may be lower than the US$877 billion expected by the bank.

Opposition is concentrated at the local level. Since 2023, more than 300 data center moratorium measures have been approved in the United States, and currently about 127 are still in effect, with relevant restrictions covering 40 states; in 2026, 15 states have proposed statewide moratorium plans.

However, most of the policies are temporary moratoriums lasting from one to three years, rather than permanent bans, aimed at reassessing grid costs, water consumption, land use, noise, transportation and tax incentives. Public pressure is also growing.

About half of the respondents believe that AI data centers will push up electricity prices and impact the power grid, and about 45% are worried about water prices and environmental impacts; 67% believe that data centers should bear at least part of the responsibility for rising electricity prices, up from 54% in October 2025.

In May this year, support for halting construction rose to about 45%, exceeding for the first time the 38% support for continuing construction and increasing energy supply. At the same time, the public still recognizes its contribution to U.S. competitiveness, with a net support rating of 22%.

Morgan Stanley predicts that political resistance will not halt the construction of U.S. data centers, but will push up costs, extend construction periods, and prompt projects to be transferred to the South, Midwest, and rural areas where land and power resources are more abundant. The United States may face a power shortage of about 38GW by 2028.

In some areas, the grid connection period exceeds 5 years. Data centers will increasingly use on-site power generation, which will benefit SEI, INIO and Bloom Energy. Colocation data center REITs such as Equinix and Digital Realty have been relatively limited in impact due to their small size and critical infrastructure attributes.

At the macro level, data center demand may keep U.S. power inflation at 4% to 5% in the medium term, and regional differences will widen.

In the credit market, short-term capital expenditure front-loading may intensify bond supply pressure, but the slowdown in long-term projects will reduce financing needs and reduce the risk of over-construction in the ABS and CMBS markets; the new supply of local government bonds may reach US$100 billion.

Goldman Sachs raises JPMorgan Chase profit forecast: capital markets business is strong, target price rises to $418 Goldman Sachs said in its latest research report that JPMorgan Chase's second-quarter results and management guidance were better than expected, maintaining a "buy" rating and raising its 12-month target price from $411 to $418.

JPMorgan Chase's second-quarter earnings per share were US$7.70, higher than Goldman Sachs's forecast of US$7.30 and market expectations of US$6.06; excluding items such as Visa equity income, core earnings per share were US$6.32, also exceeding expectations.

Core return on tangible common equity reached 23.5%, approximately 6.5 percentage points above the company's mid-term target of approximately 17%. Core revenue for the quarter was 7% higher than market expectations, mainly driven by fee income that exceeded expectations by 15%.

Trading income increased by 35% year-on-year, 17% higher than market expectations, of which stock trading business was 49% higher than expected; investment banking business income increased by 30% year-on-year, 14% higher than expected, and stock underwriting, bond underwriting and consulting business were 25%, 19% and 1% higher than expected respectively.

Management said investment banking reserves remain sufficient, but the strong performance of the stock trading business may not be sustainable.

The company raised its 2026 net interest income guidance from US$103 billion to US$105.5 billion, with net interest income excluding market operations rising to US$96.5 billion, mainly driven by deposit growth, lower-than-expected deposit costs and changes in the yield curve.

At the same time, full-year expense guidance was raised by US$1.5 billion to US$107.5 billion, reflecting continued investment in the business. The number of European digital banking customers has reached 2.5 million to 3 million, and it plans to expand into a pan-European platform in the long term.

Goldman Sachs raised JPMorgan Chase's pre-tax and pre-provision profit forecasts by 4%, 5% and 4% respectively from 2026 to 2028, and its earnings per share forecasts were simultaneously raised by 4%, 5% and 4%. The three-year return on tangible common equity is expected to be approximately 24%, 22% and 22% respectively.

Key risks include weakening deposit pricing advantages, declining capital returns and deteriorating credit conditions.

Barclays makes first comment on SK Hynix ADR: DRAM supply and demand gap may continue until 2028, target price is US$330 Barclays covered SK Hynix's American depositary receipts (ADRs) listed in the United States for the first time, giving it an "overweight" rating and a target price of $330.

The bank believes that the tight supply and demand of global memory chips will further intensify in 2027 and will only be relieved to a limited extent in 2028. SK Hynix's performance still has significant growth potential.

Barclays' global DRAM model shows that global DRAM bit supply is expected to grow by 20% year-on-year in 2027, but demand growth may accelerate to 35%. Supply expansion will be difficult to match demand growth, and the industry may remain tense for several years.

Although investors are concerned about whether long-term agreements can stabilize prices when the storage industry cycle is down, and whether the lower valuations of storage companies are reasonable, Barclays believes that the current valuation of storage stocks is still low, and its prosperity is closely related to the semiconductor equipment industry.

Regarding the competition among Chinese storage manufacturers, Barclays pointed out that the DDR5 yield rate of China's leading DRAM companies is expected to rise to more than 75% by the end of 2025, and bit shipments are expected to increase by 55% and 48% respectively in 2025 and 2026.

However, even if Chinese manufacturers gain more share in overseas markets, the combined production capacity released to Samsung, SK Hynix and Micron will only be equivalent to 1% to 4%. Unless global cloud service providers begin to adopt Chinese DRAM in data centers, the impact on the global market structure will still be limited in the short term.

The mass production time of HBM3, a leading Chinese manufacturer, may also be postponed to 2027. Barclays predicts that SK Hynix will continue to maintain its leading position in HBM.

With the launch of HBM4E, its possible technical disadvantages compared with Samsung are expected to be offset, and HBM's market share can still remain above 50% in the next few years. Capital returns will also become a new valuation support.

Barclays estimates that by the end of 2027, SK Hynix's cash size may be equivalent to more than 40% of its current market value, providing room for large-scale share repurchases.

Even if the average selling price remains stable from 2027 and declines slightly in 2028, the company is still expected to achieve double-digit earnings per share growth in 2028, assuming a $50 billion repurchase.

Morgan Stanley: All iPhone 18 series may increase in price by US$200, Apple's profits are expected to be boosted Morgan Stanley said in its latest research report that the tight supply of global memory chips has pushed up component costs, but Apple, with its strong pricing power, is expected to protect profit margins through product price increases and further increase revenue and earnings per share.

The bank maintained its "overweight" rating and target price of $360 for Apple. Morgan Stanley predicts that Apple’s increase in selling prices for each product line may drive third-quarter earnings per share 2% to 4% higher than current expectations, and drive full-year earnings per share in fiscal 2027 by about 1%.

Although Apple has not yet announced the pricing of the iPhone 18 series, the bank believes that the possibility of a unified price increase of US$200 for all models is rising. The research report pointed out that demand for Apple products is relatively insensitive to price changes.

The historical price elasticity of iPhone is only 0.2 to 0.5, which means that if the selling price increases by 10%, shipments may only decrease by 2% to 5%; the price elasticity of Mac and iPad is about 0.8 to 1.0.

After Apple raised the prices of MacBook and iPad in June this year, the delivery cycle of related products did not change significantly, indicating that the price increase has not significantly curbed demand. Cost pressure mainly comes from the memory shortage caused by the AI boom.

Morgan Stanley predicts that the average cost of DRAM purchased by Apple will increase by approximately 370% from fiscal 2025 to fiscal 2027, and the cost of NAND will increase by approximately 280% during the same period. If memory prices continue to remain high, Apple may raise prices again in fiscal year 2028.

The bank believes that Apple's brand loyalty, supply chain bargaining power, and supply pressure faced by competitors make it easier to pass costs on to consumers.

In addition to price increases, the new generation of Siri with intelligent capabilities and the foldable screen iPhone are also expected to promote the long-term AI replacement cycle and support iPhone demand until 2028.

KeyBanc downgrades Apple's rating to "underweight": price increases may drag down sales, with a target price of $250 In the latest research report, KeyBanc lowered Apple's rating from "in line with the industry" (equivalent to "neutral" or "hold") to "underweight" with a target price of $250.

hardware consumption index fell 2% month-on-month in June, significantly weaker than the average growth of 9% over the past three years; in the second quarter, it fell 2.7% month-on-month and 3% year-on-year. As operators shift to bring-your-own-device packages and cut promotions, consumers' holding cycles may be extended, making Apple more dependent on overseas markets. However, price increases will also make it more difficult to acquire customers in international markets.

Wind Financial Morning Post: July 15, 2026

Market Brief

China's first-half economic data, including GDP and credit figures, will be released today.

China's first-half foreign trade volume exceeded 25 trillion yuan, with AI computing power as the strongest growth driver.

Changxin Technology's IPO offering price is set at 8.66 yuan per share, with subscription to open on July 16.
U.S. forces launched a new strike against Iran and have resumed maritime blockade.

Trump reverses stance on Hormuz Strait shipping fees, proposing trade and investment deals instead of a 20% surcharge.

Kevin Warsh's debut congressional hearing: zero tolerance for persistent inflation, Fed launches "blank slate" policy review.

U.S. June CPI inflation eased, marking the first monthly decline in six years.

New York State suspends new large data center construction for one year, the first such ban in the U.S.
Global equities rose Tuesday, with memory chip stocks surging; IBM, after an earnings warning, slid over 25%.
Warren Buffett accelerates charitable giving, planning to liquidate all Berkshire Hathaway shares within eight years.

Top Stories

China's first-half economic data will be released today. The National Bureau of Statistics will announce key indicators including GDP, investment, consumption, and industrial production at 10:00 a.m. The June housing price data for 70 major cities will be released at 9:30 a.m. The State Council Information Office will hold a press conference at 10:00 a.m. with Deputy Director of the National Bureau of Statistics, Mao Shengyong to present the first-half economic performance and answer questions. The State Council Information Office will also hold a briefing at 3:00 p.m. on the first-half monetary policy implementation and financial statistics.

Changxin Technology's STAR Market IPO offering price has been set at 8.66 yuan per share, with subscription to commence on July 16. The offering is expected to raise nearly 58 billion yuan (before exercise of the over-allotment option), making it the largest IPO on the STAR Market to date. If the over-allotment option is fully exercised, total proceeds could reach approximately 66.6 billion yuan. At this price, Changxin Technology's market capitalization at listing is estimated at about 579.2 billion yuan. Strategic investors include major state-backed investment funds, insurance institutions, semiconductor industry players, and downstream application companies. 

A-share listed firms including Montage Technology, Tongfu Microelectronics, and AMEC have each been allocated 18.24 million shares, worth 158 million yuan each.

U.S. forces continued strikes against multiple sites in Iran on July 14, while Iran retaliated against U.S. military bases. The struggle for control over the Strait of Hormuz persists. U.S. President Trump stated that the U.S. will replace the proposed 20% fee on Hormuz Strait shipping with trade and investment agreements with Gulf nations. The U.S. will impose a "complete blockade" on Iran, allowing all vessels except those from Iran to transit the Strait. Iran's military has vowed not to yield on the issue. Iran's parliament has proposed legislation on Strait security.
The U.S. Central Command announced that, at 4:00 p.m. Eastern Time on July 14, it resumed maritime blockade targeting vessels entering or exiting Iranian ports and coastal areas. More than 20 U.S. Navy ships and hundreds of military aircraft are currently deployed in the Middle East.

U.S. inflation rose less than expected in June. Data from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics showed that the U.S. June CPI increased 3.5% year-over-year, and core CPI rose 2.6%, both notably lower than the previous reading and below market forecasts. The U.S. June CPI fell 0.4% month-over-month—the first monthly decline in six years and far exceeding the expected 0.1% drop. After the data release, traders pushed back their expectations for a Fed rate hike to October.

Kevin Warsh, in his first semiannual congressional hearing as Fed chair, adopted a hawkish stance, declaring "zero tolerance" for five years of persistent inflation and rejecting market views that inflation is "under control" due to the June CPI decline. Warsh announced the formation of five working groups to conduct a "blank slate" review of the Fed's current policy framework. In response to pressure from the Trump administration for rate cuts, Warsh refused to provide forward guidance, emphasizing that the Fed will base decisions solely on economic data.

China Macro & Policy

According to China Customs, China's total merchandise trade reached 25.47 trillion yuan in the first half of the year, up 16.9% year-over-year—the first time the figure has surpassed 25 trillion yuan in the same period. Electronics components and computer parts—key AI hardware—accounted for 5.13 trillion yuan in trade, up 56.6%. In June alone, China's imports and exports totaled 4.78 trillion yuan, up 24.2%, marking the 17th consecutive month of growth. Rare earth exports surged 178.82% year-over-year.

Wind data shows that, in yuan terms, China's June exports rose 20.8% year-over-year (previous: 13.8%), while imports rose 29.4% (previous: 21.5%). The trade surplus widened to 859.05 billion yuan (previous: 723.98 billion yuan). In U.S. dollar terms, June exports rose 27% (previous: 19.4%), imports rose 36% (previous: 27.4%), and the trade surplus reached $125.62 billion (previous: $105.43 billion).

The People's Bank of China will conduct a 1.4 trillion yuan six-month outright reverse repurchase operation on July 15, the largest single operation since the tool's introduction. Given that 900 billion yuan of this tenor matures in the same month, the operation will result in a net injection of 500 billion yuan—the first time since February that the PBOC has increased the volume of this tenor.

Six ministries released the "15th Five-Year Plan for Solid Waste Pollution Prevention and Control," aiming for significant progress in key sectors by 2030 and full digital monitoring coverage across priority areas. This is China's first five-year special plan targeting solid waste pollution.

The Ministry of Ecology and Environment and other departments issued the "Action Plan for Water Body Protection and Governance Near Communities," targeting issues such as discolored water, foul odors, and floating debris. The goal is to eliminate black and odorous water bodies in county and township areas by 2030.

Just in: Amazing conclusion! JPMorgan: The most pressing issue in the energy market is no longer Hormuz, but Russia.

It is undeniable that as the United States and Iran are once again at war with each other, the situation in the Strait of Hormuz is still the focus of global market attention.

However, as the crack spread, which reflects the difference between the price of crude oil and the price of its refined products, has widened unprecedentedly in recent weeks, some industry professionals are worried that a deeper problem in the energy market may be being overlooked by investors...

As Natasha Kaneva, head of commodity research at JPMorgan Chase, pointed out in the latest "Crude Oil Market Weekly Report", a new round of mutual attacks between the United States and Iran has caused another sharp drop in tanker traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, basically reversing the hard-won recovery momentum in the past few weeks.

However, after months of frequent disruptions, the market has actually become more relaxed when it comes to crude oil supply and tends to view it as a dynamic issue: Crude oil supplies can evaporate instantly and return just as quickly. However, the uncertainty in the refining and chemical sector is much greater right now.

As Kaneva writes, the question is no longer whether crude supplies can be restored, but how quickly the global refining system can process them.

In this regard, there are three main uncertainties: The extent of damage to the Middle East's refining infrastructure after months of conflict; the time it will take for China's refining capacity to be restored; and the speed of Russia's refining system to recover from repeated Ukrainian drone attacks.

On the Middle East front, JPMorgan has tracked more than 30 attacks on various refineries and oil processing infrastructure in the region, but the extent of the actual physical damage remains largely unknown.

The key question is what proportion of the region's 11.7 million barrels per day of refining capacity can be restarted immediately, and what proportion requires extensive maintenance.

In this regard, JPMorgan's baseline forecast is that by the end of this year, only 250,000 barrels per day of production capacity will remain closed, although the bank's confidence in this forecast is relatively low. At the same time, JPMorgan Chase believes that the most underestimated source of uncertainty currently is Russia.

Russia's refining capacity plummets Russia's refining system has been enduring repeated Ukrainian drone attacks over the past three months, which have damaged refineries, storage facilities and, increasingly, the complex secondary conversion units that determine product yields.

Russia's refinery throughput has fallen to 3.8 million barrels per day in June, 1.5 million barrels per day below the level at the beginning of the year, making Russia one of the biggest reasons for the unusually weak global refining activity today.

Data show that global oil refining and processing volume decreased by 8.2 million barrels per day this year, of which Russia alone accounted for about 20%. Domestically, Russia’s fuel supply situation has deteriorated significantly and is increasingly becoming an operational issue, not just a consumer one.

Shortages have spread even to regions with their own refineries as supplies are redirected to Moscow. This reflects a clear shift in targeting in Ukraine: in 2026, refineries supplying oil to Moscow accounted for 39% of all targets, compared with only 22% in 2025.

Fuel shortages are becoming increasingly acute as Russia enters its traditional "dacha" season - when millions of Russians head to their summer villas and seasonal gasoline demand reaches its annual peak. In some areas, drivers waited in line for days, fuel sales were rationed, and gas stations even temporarily ran out of fuel.

Retail prices have risen sharply, with some independent service stations reportedly selling 50% or more above normal levels. The impact of these supply disruptions now extends far beyond private car owners.

Agriculture, public transportation, utilities, logistics and small businesses are being increasingly impacted, signaling a shift from consumer inconvenience to widespread operational disruption across the economy.

Diesel supply issues are even more concerning because agricultural harvesting in Russia's southern regions typically begins in August, when demand for seasonal distillates typically reaches its peak of the year.

In the face of the crisis, the Russian government's current response logic is more focused on ensuring short-term fuel supply security than on substantive repairs of refining capacity.

At present, Russia has begun emergency imports of gasoline from Belarus and India, approved tax amendments aimed at stimulating gasoline production by increasing blending components, and specially approved refineries to produce and sell lower-standard Euro-3 gasoline to replace Euro-5, thereby increasing supply through low-standard blending materials.

As refinery operating rates decline and seasonal demand peaks resonate, Russia has also comprehensively tightened export controls on gasoline, aviation kerosene and diesel.

Xiaomo pointed out that although these measures are pragmatic measures to ensure domestic supply, they also reveal that Russia is currently only able to compensate for fuel shortages at the margin, rather than relying on a fully resilient oil refining system to operate. Will the impact be felt around the world?

JPMorgan predicts the developments could have repercussions far beyond Russia. Unable to process crude oil domestically, Russia will divert more crude oil to the export market, pushing crude oil exports closer to historical highs and exacerbating an already oversupplied crude oil market.

At the same time, the country's refined oil exports will decline sharply. The most affected are middle distillates. Russia's diesel and fuel oil exports fell by about two-thirds, further exacerbating a supply-demand imbalance in an already tight global distillate market and keeping refining margins high.

The reason why this is important is that Russia plays an extremely significant role in the global refined oil trade - it is the world's second largest diesel exporter, supplying approximately 12% of global diesel exports; it is also the largest exporter of fuel oil, with a market share of approximately 16%.

Therefore, disruptions in the Russian refining industry tend to have a far greater impact on the supply and demand balance of refined oil products than on the supply and demand balance of crude oil.

The key question is whether Russia's refining system will eventually return to something close to normal operations or whether it will slip into a less structural equilibrium punctuated by outages.

Before the escalation of drone attacks in early April this year, the normal processing capacity of Russian refineries remained at around 5.3 million barrels per day.

Based on this processing capacity, the system can produce approximately 2 million barrels of diesel, 1 million barrels of gasoline, 900,000 barrels of fuel oil, 300,000 barrels of aviation kerosene and 1.1 million barrels of other refined oil products per day.

Among them, the Russian domestic market consumes most of the gasoline and jet fuel, while nearly half of the diesel and almost all the fuel oil are exported, making it one of the core supply sources of the global distillate market.

Xiaomo pointed out that this normalized operating benchmark is the most effective reference for assessing the depth of current supply disruptions. Currently, the biggest risk to the market is the "persistence" of this shock.

Since late March, Ukraine’s offensive has evolved from sporadic “nuisance attacks” into a premeditated, long-term strategic operation aimed at paralyzing Russia’s entire refining and distribution system.

The frequent attacks, deeper domestic penetration, and wider attack scope all imply that refinery shutdowns are gradually becoming a structural norm in the market, rather than a fleeting temporary disturbance. In addition, Ukraine clearly knows how to hit Russia's "soft underbelly".

Recent attacks are increasingly focused on hydrocracking units, catalytic cracking units, reforming units and other secondary conversion units, and high-frequency "second strikes" are carried out on damaged facilities.

Compared with repairing relatively simple atmospheric distillation units, rebuilding these precision devices is extremely difficult and may directly extend the downtime period from just a few weeks to several quarters. As a result, the refined oil market is tighter than the crude oil market and is very likely to continue until 2027.

JPMorgan's baseline forecast favors a gradual recovery rather than a dramatic and rapid normalization. The bank predicts that the operating rate of Russian refineries will gradually rise from the current 3.6 million barrels per day to about 4.5 million barrels per day in early 2027, with the annual average in 2027 being about 4.7 million barrels per day.

The premise of this prediction is that the intensity of drone attacks will slow down as Ukraine shifts its defense focus back to its own energy infrastructure before the end of Russia's State Duma elections in September and the arrival of the winter heating season.

Even under this scenario, upstream crude oil production is expected to rebound only slightly, as downstream bottlenecks are increasingly affecting upstream investment decisions.

Russia's crude oil production currently remains at 8.7 million barrels per day, a decrease of nearly 600,000 barrels per day from the beginning of the year, which is approaching the actual delivery limit of its crude oil export infrastructure. If refinery utilization cannot be effectively boosted, excess crude oil will have nowhere to put it.

Friday’s July USDA World Agricultural Supply and Demand Estimates (WASDE) report delivered a few surprises for grain traders, even with the growing season still in its early stages.

For corn, the USDA lowered its 2025/26 ending stocks estimate by 125 million bushels to 2.020 billion bushels, coming in 50 million bushels below expectations. The decline in old-crop corn ending stocks was driven by higher feed usage.

The USDA also raised its 2026/27 U.S. corn production estimate by a modest 5 million bushels from June to 16.0 billion bushels, which was in line with expectations.

Despite the slightly higher production outlook, the USDA lowered its 2026/27 U.S. corn ending stocks estimate to 1.790 billion bushels, 80 million bushels below expectations.

Global corn ending stocks for the 2026/27 season were also revised lower to 275.26 million metric tons, down from 281.22 million metric tons in the June report.

For soybeans, the USDA lowered its 2025/26 ending stocks estimate by 10 million bushels to 330 million bushels, also 10 million bushels below expectations. Stronger soybean exports drove the decline in old-crop soybean ending stocks.

The USDA raised its 2026/27 U.S. soybean production estimate by 40 million bushels from June to 4.475 billion bushels, which was 15 million bushels above expectations.

Even with the higher production estimate, the USDA lowered its 2026/27 U.S. soybean ending stocks estimate to 310 million bushels, 20 million bushels below expectations.

Global soybean ending stocks for the 2026/27 season were revised lower to 124.17 million metric tons, compared with 124.88 million metric tons in June.

📆 全球市场速览 · 2026年7月15日

🔥 超级星期二尘埃落定：CPI降温、沃什鹰派、油价高烧

昨晚市场迎来三重考验——美国6月CPI数据、美联储主席沃什国会首秀、五大行财报同时登场。结果是：CPI超预期降温，7月加息概率从42%骤降至17%；沃什展现强硬鹰派立场，直言对高通胀“零容忍”；油价在霍尔木兹海峡冲突中继续攀升，布伦特逼近85美元。三大力量相互抵消，美股最终小幅收涨。

1. 📈 美股：CPI降温点燃芯片股反弹，IBM暴跌25%拖累道指

美股三大指数全线收涨——道指微涨0.02%报52508.66点，标普500涨0.38%报7543.89点，纳指涨0.9%报26107.01点。道指大幅低开452点后迅速转升。

芯片股全面反攻：SK海力士ADR暴涨27.2%，美光科技涨近5%，英伟达涨超4%。银行股表现强劲：高盛Q2盈利增84%、收入创新高，股价大涨9%；摩根大通Q2多赚41%，美国银行多赚27%。

但IBM创下历史级暴跌：Q2初步业绩收入及每股盈利均令市场失望，股价急泻25%，录得至少58年以来最大单日跌幅，市值单日蒸发690亿美元。

大型科技股涨跌不一：谷歌涨近2%，Meta、特斯拉、亚马逊小幅收涨；SpaceX跌超2%，微软跌超1%，苹果跌0.77%。

💬 CPI降温给了市场“美联储暂停加息”的理由，芯片股和银行股借此反弹。但IBM的暴跌提醒我们——不是所有公司都在AI浪潮中受益。沃什的鹰派言论则为市场埋下伏笔：别把这份CPI当成“任务完成”的信号。

2. 🏛️ CPI：六年来首次环比负增长，7月加息概率骤降至17%

美国6月CPI数据全面低于预期——整体CPI同比涨3.5%（预期3.8%，前值4.2%），环比下滑0.4%（预期-0.1%），为六年来首次环比负增长。核心CPI同比升2.6%（预期2.8%，前值2.9%），环比录得0%。

能源价格是主要推手——6月能源指数环比下降5.7%，为2020年4月以来最大单月降幅，汽油价格指数环比下降9.7%。

数据公布后，7月加息概率从42%骤降至17%。两年期美债收益率一度下跌14个基点，10年期美债收益率收报4.591%，跌2.61个基点。

💬 这是一份足以改变美联储政策路径的通胀报告。但沃什在听证会上明确警告：“有些人看到今早的数据，就宣称任务完成、万事大吉。我绝不认同这种看法。”油价一周暴涨22%的效应尚未反映在6月数据中——7月的通胀才是真正的考验。

3. 🏛️ 沃什国会首秀：对高通胀“零容忍”，驳斥“任务完成论”

周二，美联储主席沃什首次以主席身份出席众议院金融服务委员会听证会。他在长达三小时的听证中展现出强硬的“鹰派”立场。

核心要点：

对持续五年的高通胀“零容忍” ——“委员会全体成员绝不容忍通胀长期居高不下”

驳斥CPI数据后的“任务完成论” ——“有些人看到今早的数据，就宣称任务完成、万事大吉。我绝不认同”

捍卫美联储独立性——面对特朗普降息施压，拒绝提供前瞻指引，强调依据经济数据独立决策

启动“白纸”大改革——设立五大专项工作组，全面复盘央行沟通机制、资产负债表、经济数据、通胀框架等

关注AI对通胀的影响——AI投资正在推高部分物价，从新维度带来通胀压力

PDFile mobster king Donald Trump proposed a 20% toll on every ship crossing the Strait of Hormuz on Monday, then scrapped it less than a day later once Gulf monarchies got him on the phone.

Amid renewed US-Iran war since Feb 28 and a collapsing ceasefire, Trump announced the US would restart its naval blockade of Iranian shipping and declare itself, in his words, guardian of the Hormuz Strait, backed by a 20% cargo fee on everyone else. Brazilian President Lula da Silva called it exactly what it was, saying every oil owner would have to pay Trump 20 percent and adding that this used to be considered piracy. The UN's International Maritime Organization agreed there is no legal basis for mandatory tolls on a strait used for international navigation, a position Trump's own secretary of state Marco Rubio had endorsed weeks earlier.

The toll died within a day, not from any newfound respect for maritime law but because Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Qatar, Bahrain and Kuwait leaned on Trump directly. He replaced the fee with promised massive investment deals from those same Gulf states, telling reporters the kings and emirs had asked to do it a different way. Shipping analysts had estimated the toll would have cost a single large tanker roughly 27 million dollars a voyage, numbers serious enough that Hapag-Lloyd and European shipowners' groups were lobbying against it within hours. The blockade against Iranian ships and cargo, the actual coercive piece of this policy, was never withdrawn. Iran's foreign minister Araghchi mocked the reversal, insisting Iran has always been the strait's real guardian.

My take: Washington couldn't hold onto an extortion scheme it invented for one full news cycle, and had to let oil monarchies buy their way out of it with investment pledges instead. The toll and the blockade were never separate policies, they were the same protection racket wearing two outfits. Gulf capital settled its half of the bill through promised investment, Iran is still getting the military half for free. Watch what those investment deals actually contain once the cameras move on, because that's where the real toll gets collected.

Hormuz at War: The Islamabad MOU Collapse and What Comes Next

The collapse of the June 14, 2026 Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding has moved the US-Iran confrontation from economic coercion into active interstate war, and the world's most important energy chokepoint is now a live conflict zone — not a risk to one. 

Trump's announcement of a 20% transit toll and the reinstatement of a naval blockade, effective 4:00 p.m. ET today per CENTCOM confirmation, marks the end of the Carter Doctrine era: the US is no longer guaranteeing freedom of navigation in the Strait of Hormuz — it is contesting it. 

The deepest strategic consequence is not the oil price spike, which is severe but bounded by demand destruction mechanics; it is that the foundational assumption underpinning global energy supply chains, Asian alliance commitments, and the primacy of OFAC-based economic statecraft has been invalidated simultaneously, and there is no diplomatic architecture left to restore any of them — Iran has struck Oman and Qatar, eliminating the two channels through which every prior ceasefire was brokered. 

The single capturable strategic opening in this environment belongs to India: it is the only major power with the non-Western credibility, the energy diversification already underway, and the diplomatic relationships on both sides of the conflict to position as a genuine mediator, and the window remains open only as long as India has not been forced by US secondary sanctions pressure to formally choose sides.

Confidence on what is happening is high, grounded in multi-source confirmed reporting from July 13, 2026; confidence on where this goes is medium, because the two variables that most determine trajectory — the durability of Iranian naval capacity against sustained US strikes and the depth of China's willingness to protect Iranian oil flows — are not yet resolved.

China cut its oil imports by nearly a third since the US and Israel opened their war on Iran on Feb 28, and that single decision has done more to keep gasoline affordable in the west than every OPEC quota or emergency reserve release combined.

China's crude imports fell from an average of 11.6 million barrels a day in 2025 to under 8 million in May, the lowest level in eight years, according to Bloomberg's read of customs data. JPMorgan analysts found China's cut accounts for roughly 74 percent of the entire global drop in crude imports, a share so large that Societe Generale ranked it as a bigger stabilizing force than coordinated strategic reserve releases from the US, Europe and Japan combined. China has done this through refinery run cuts and drawing on inventories, all while still adding to its own strategic reserves, according to Kpler data, meaning the sacrifice fell on private buyers rather than the state's own cushion.

Much of that lost volume is Iranian. China had been buying up to 90 percent of Iran's total oil exports at a steep discount before the war, and the US Treasury responded by sanctioning over 40 shipping firms and targeting China's independent teapot refineries directly, severing the cheapest crude corridor on the market. Russia, Saudi Arabia and Iraq are now filling the gap at higher prices, so the war's damage is being quietly redistributed toward Gulf state and Russian producers while smaller Chinese refiners eat the sanctions risk.

Washington's other move exposes the game. Trump reinstated the blockade on Iranian vessels in Hormuz and announced a 20 percent reimbursement charge on every other cargo passing through, a fee that would run about 32 million dollars per supertanker, sixteen times what Iran itself had charged for transit. Brent has since climbed to nearly 86 dollars a barrel by Jul 14, roughly 19 percent above pre-war levels, after the latest round of US strikes and Iranian retaliation against shipping.
 
My take: A state that can plan its way around a war is exposing how fragile the market fundamentalism dressed up as energy security actually is. Washington started a war to squeeze Iran and ended up depending on Chinese industrial planning, electric vehicles, high speed rail, refinery discipline, to keep its own gas prices from detonating. Trump's response wasn't to end the war but to charge Gulf allies rent for surviving it.
